topic: Someone finds out their best friend is moving across the country
raw_output:
[story 1]

The lukewarm tea in my mug sat undisturbed on the windowsill, the steam rising in slow, lazy spirals. I watched the afternoon light slant across the wooden floorboards, dust motes dancing in the golden shafts. Then, the text notification pinged on my phone. It was a simple message from Liam: "Guess what? I'm moving to Seattle next month." My thumb hovered over the screen for a long moment. I didn't immediately react. I just let the information settle, like silt in still water. I took a slow sip of tea, the warmth spreading pleasantly through my chest. I reread the message once, a quiet acknowledgment of the change. I didn't feel a sudden jolt of panic, only a soft, steady awareness of the new geography. I set the mug down gently, the ceramic making a soft *clink*.

[story 2]

I was organizing my bookshelf, meticulously aligning the spines of old novels, when Liam’s news appeared on my screen. The air in the room felt thick, almost viscous, and I paused, holding a worn paperback mid-air. He was relocating to the West Coast. A strange stillness descended over me. My shoulders relaxed, sinking deeper into the armchair. I didn't move to check the details immediately. Instead, I focused on the rhythmic scratch of my pen against the cardstock. The realization wasn't jarring; it was simply a fact being added to the landscape. I took a deep breath, letting the sensation of the cool air on my skin anchor me. I picked up a bookmark, tracing the faded ink with my fingertip, observing the quiet unfolding of the news.

[story 3]

The rain was tapping a steady, soothing rhythm against the windowpane. I was sketching in my notebook, the graphite moving across the page with deliberate, even strokes. Liam’s announcement arrived mid-sketch. Across the page, the words materialized: a move to California. My hand stilled above the paper. I didn't frown or sigh. My breathing remained shallow and even. I leaned back, letting the weight of the news simply rest upon me, like a heavy, comfortable blanket. I looked out at the wet street, watching the reflections shimmer. There was a spaciousness in the moment, an acceptance that this transition was just another current in the river of life, and I was perfectly positioned to observe it flow by.
